personality_voice.book_baseline: In the book corpus for HBP, Sirius is already dead before the story begins. His personality is conveyed entirely through retrospective references: Harry's grief and longing (Scene 54: 'it's just hard to realize he won't write to me again'), Dumbledore's characterization of him as someone who would want Harry to keep fighting (Scene 56-57), Slughorn's offhand description of him as a 'talented boy' who broke from his family's Slytherin tradition (Scene 47), Snape's detention records showing him as a rule-breaker and troublemaker alongside James (Scene 218-219), and Harry's memory of him 'prowling its dark musty rooms alone, imprisoned within the place he had wanted so desperately to leave' (Scene 28). The books across the full series establish Sirius as fiercely loyal, impulsive, reckless, emotionally intense, prone to bitterness from his Azkaban years, deeply loving toward Harry as a surrogate son, and defined by his hatred of confinement and injustice. He is also shown to have flaws: cruelty toward Kreacher, recklessness that contributed to his own death, and a tendency to treat Harry as a stand-in for James.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: In the HBP film corpus, Sirius appears only as a name referenced in passing. Slughorn mentions him briefly (Film Scene 4): 'Ah yes. Regulus Black. You no doubt know of his older brother Sirius. Died a few weeks ago. I taught the whole Black family except Sirius. Shame. Talented boy.' There is no other direct characterization of Sirius in the film corpus. His personality, voice, and behavioral patterns are entirely absent from the film material provided.
personality_voice.difference: The film corpus for HBP contains no scenes in which Sirius's personality or voice can be evaluated directly. The single reference to him by Slughorn is present in both book and film (Scene 47 book / Film Scene 4), so that element is preserved. However, the rich retrospective texture the books provide — Harry's grief over the lost letters, Dumbledore's characterization of Sirius as someone who would want Harry to fight on, Snape's detention records evoking Sirius as a troublemaker — is absent from the film corpus. The film does not include the equivalent of book Scenes 52-57 (Dumbledore consoling Harry about Sirius, Harry invoking Sirius's spirit as motivation) or Scene 218-219 (Snape's detention records referencing Sirius and James). These absences mean the film fails to reinforce Sirius's personality traits even posthumously.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: Because Sirius is dead in HBP, his personality can only be conveyed through how others remember and reference him. The book does this extensively and meaningfully. The film reduces this to a single throwaway line. The core traits — loyalty, recklessness, his bond with Harry, his identity as a rule-breaker — are not reinforced in the film. This is a meaningful reduction, though not a distortion of what little is shown. Score reflects that the one reference present is accurate but the broader posthumous characterization is largely absent.
personality_voice.evidence_status: Directly evidenced for what is present; reasonably inferred for what is absent based on comparison of book scenes 52-57, 218-219 against the film corpus.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: In HBP, Sirius's narrative role is posthumous but substantial. He functions as: (1) a plot driver — his death triggers Harry's grief arc and his will bequeaths Grimmauld Place and Kreacher to Harry, which has direct plot consequences (Scenes 25-36); (2) a moral compass — Harry repeatedly invokes what Sirius would have wanted as motivation for his own choices (Scenes 56, 63, 140); (3) a source of conflict — the question of Kreacher's loyalty, Mundungus stealing from Grimmauld Place (Scenes 133-140), and Bellatrix potentially inheriting the house (Scenes 30-31) all flow from Sirius's death and legacy; (4) a relational anchor — his death affects Tonks's Patronus and emotional state (Scenes 65-67, 98-99, 187-193), and Harry's relationship with Snape is intensified by Sirius's death (Scenes 105-106). Sirius thus has significant posthumous agency: his will, his possessions, and his memory actively shape events.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: In the film corpus, Sirius's posthumous narrative role is reduced to a single name-drop by Slughorn. The inheritance plot (Grimmauld Place, Kreacher), the Mundungus theft subplot, the Tonks grief arc, and Harry's repeated invocations of Sirius as moral motivation are all absent from the film corpus provided. His death does not visibly drive any plot element shown in the film scenes.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The film removes or does not show the following narrative functions that Sirius performs in the book: the inheritance/will scene with Dumbledore, the Kreacher ownership transfer, the Mundungus theft of Black family heirlooms, Harry's use of Sirius's memory as motivation, and the Tonks grief subplot. These are not minor texture — they are plot-driving and character-defining functions. The single Slughorn reference does not substitute for any of them.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Sirius's posthumous agency — his will, his possessions, his memory as moral driver — is a genuine narrative function in the books. The film corpus shows none of this. This is a significant reduction in narrative role. The transfer rule applies: Harry's motivation speech invoking Sirius (book Scene 56) has no equivalent in the film corpus. The Kreacher ownership scene, which is a direct consequence of Sirius's death and shapes later plot, is absent.
narrative_role_agency.evidence_status: Directly evidenced by absence in film corpus; book baseline directly evidenced from Scenes 25-36, 56, 105-106, 133-140.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Sirius's motivations in HBP are conveyed posthumously. His core motivation — fierce love for Harry and desire to protect him — is referenced through Harry's grief (Scene 54: Sirius was 'almost like a parent') and Dumbledore's acknowledgment (Scene 53: 'a brutal ending to what should have been a long and happy relationship'). His internal conflict — the tension between his desire to act and his confinement, which contributed to his reckless dash to the Ministry — is referenced obliquely through Harry's analysis of Snape's taunts (Scene 105-106). His will leaving everything to Harry reflects his motivation to provide for and protect his godson even in death.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film corpus provides no material from which Sirius's motivations or internal conflicts can be evaluated. The single Slughorn reference is purely biographical, not motivational.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film does not convey Sirius's motivations — his love for Harry, his desire to act, his conflict between caution and recklessness — even in the posthumous register the books use. The scenes that establish these (Dumbledore's consolation of Harry, Harry's reflection on Snape's role in Sirius's death) are absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Because the corpus is so limited on this dimension, confidence is low. The absence of motivational material is penalized moderately — the film does not contradict Sirius's motivations, but it also does not convey them. This is a meaningful gap given that the books use HBP extensively to retrospectively illuminate why Sirius acted as he did.
motivations_internal_conflict.evidence_status: Not enough evidence in film corpus; book baseline reasonably inferred from Scenes 53-56, 105-106.

character_arc.book_baseline: Sirius's arc across the full series ends with his death in OotP. In HBP, his arc is complete — he is dead. What HBP does is provide a retrospective coda: Harry processes the loss, accepts it, and uses Sirius's memory as motivation to keep fighting. The 'arc' in HBP is therefore Harry's arc of grief and acceptance, with Sirius as its object. Sirius himself has no further arc. His legacy arc — what he leaves behind (the house, Kreacher, Buckbeak, his possessions) — is a form of posthumous narrative continuation.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film corpus does not show any of the legacy arc. The inheritance, Kreacher, Buckbeak, and the Mundungus subplot are absent. Harry's grief arc over Sirius, which is the primary vehicle for Sirius's posthumous presence, is not shown in the film corpus.
character_arc.difference: The posthumous legacy arc — Sirius's will, his possessions, his influence on Harry's motivation — is absent from the film. This is not a distortion of Sirius's arc (which ended in OotP) but it does remove the narrative continuation of his character into HBP.
character_arc.penalty_logic: Since Sirius's arc is technically complete before HBP begins, the score cannot be heavily penalized for arc incompleteness. However, the film's failure to show the posthumous legacy elements (which constitute his 'arc continuation' in HBP) is a meaningful reduction. The score reflects that the arc's endpoint is not distorted, but its HBP continuation is absent.
character_arc.evidence_status: Reasonably inferred from book Scenes 25-37, 56, 279-280; film corpus provides insufficient material.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Sirius's key relationships in HBP are all posthumous: (1) Harry — the central relationship, conveyed through Harry's grief, his reluctance to discuss Sirius, his use of Sirius's memory as motivation, and his horror at Bellatrix potentially inheriting the house; (2) Tonks — her grief over Sirius's death affects her Metamorphosing abilities and her emotional state, suggesting a deep bond (Scenes 65-67, 98-99, 190-193); (3) Snape — Harry's hatred of Snape is intensified by his belief that Snape's taunts drove Sirius to his death (Scenes 105-106); (4) Kreacher — Sirius's mistreatment of Kreacher is referenced as the reason Kreacher betrayed him, and the ownership transfer is a direct consequence (Scenes 32-36).
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film corpus shows none of these relational dynamics. The Harry-Sirius relationship is not explored through grief scenes. The Tonks subplot is absent. The Snape-Sirius dynamic is not referenced. The Kreacher ownership scene is absent.
key_relationships.difference: All of Sirius's key relationships in HBP are absent from the film corpus. The single Slughorn reference does not engage with any of them.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The emotional dynamics that define Sirius's relational presence in HBP — Harry's grief, Tonks's love, Snape's antagonism, Kreacher's betrayal — are entirely absent. This is a significant loss. The score reflects that the relationships are not distorted (nothing contradicts them) but they are not present.
key_relationships.evidence_status: Directly evidenced by absence; book baseline directly evidenced from Scenes 53-56, 65-67, 98-99, 105-106, 32-36.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: HBP uses Sirius's death to add complexity to his character retrospectively: his recklessness is shown to have had fatal consequences (Scene 105-106, Harry's analysis); his love for Harry is shown to have been genuinely parental (Scene 54); his complicated relationship with Kreacher is shown to have had plot consequences (Scenes 32-36); his family legacy (the house, the Black family tradition, Bellatrix as heir) adds texture to his identity as a blood-traitor from a dark family. The Mundungus subplot (Scenes 133-140) adds the detail that even his possessions are being disrespected, which Harry finds viscerally upsetting — revealing the depth of his attachment. Slughorn's comment that he was a 'talented boy' who broke from his family's Slytherin tradition adds nuance to his identity.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film retains only the Slughorn reference, which preserves the 'talented boy who broke from family tradition' nuance. Everything else — the recklessness analysis, the parental love, the Kreacher complexity, the Mundungus subplot, the house legacy — is absent.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film loses almost all of the complexity and nuance that HBP adds to Sirius's posthumous characterization. What remains is a single biographical note that is accurate but thin.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The lost material is substantial and meaningful. The Kreacher subplot, the Mundungus theft, Harry's grief scenes, and the Snape-Sirius dynamic all add complexity that the film does not preserve. The character is reduced to a name. Score cap 4 applies: the film preserves only Sirius's broad identity (dead godfather, talented wizard, Gryffindor who broke from his family) but removes most of what defines his book characterization in HBP.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.evidence_status: Directly evidenced by comparison of book Scenes 28, 32-36, 53-56, 105-106, 133-140 against film corpus.

lost_or_transferred_material: Sirius's will and inheritance scene — Dumbledore informing Harry of the bequest of Grimmauld Place, gold, and Kreacher (book Scenes 25-36)
Kreacher ownership transfer — Harry ordering Kreacher to Hogwarts kitchens as a consequence of Sirius's death (book Scenes 32-36)
Buckbeak/Witherwings legacy — Sirius leaving Buckbeak to Harry (book Scene 37)
Harry's grief monologue about Sirius's letters — 'it's just hard to realize he won't write to me again' (book Scene 54)
Dumbledore's consolation of Harry about Sirius — 'Sirius would have been proud of you' / 'a brutal ending to what should have been a long and happy relationship' (book Scenes 52-53, 55-57)
Harry invoking Sirius's memory as motivation to keep fighting (book Scene 56)
Tonks's grief over Sirius affecting her Metamorphosing abilities (book Scenes 65-67)
Harry's speculation that Tonks was in love with Sirius / her Patronus changing (book Scenes 190-193)
Harry's analysis that Snape's taunts drove Sirius to his death — intensifying Harry-Snape conflict (book Scenes 105-106)
Mundungus stealing Black family heirlooms — Harry's visceral anger at disrespect to Sirius's possessions (book Scenes 133-140)
Bellatrix potentially inheriting Grimmauld Place as Sirius's killer — Harry's horror at this (book Scenes 30-31)
Snape's detention records referencing James Potter and Sirius Black as rule-breakers (book Scenes 218-219)
Harry's memory of Sirius 'prowling its dark musty rooms alone, imprisoned' — establishing Sirius's suffering at Grimmauld Place (book Scene 28)
Harry's reflection at Dumbledore's funeral that there was no body to bury for Sirius, and no loophole to search for (book Scene 279-280)
score_caps_applied: Cap 4 (score above 70 not permitted): The film preserves only Sirius's broad identity — dead godfather, talented wizard who broke from his Slytherin family — but removes most of what defines his book characterization in HBP: the inheritance plot, the grief arc, the Tonks relationship, the Kreacher complexity, the Mundungus subplot, and his function as Harry's moral compass.

Cap 3 (score above 75 not permitted): Multiple core book functions and emotional beats — Harry's grief scenes, the inheritance agency, the Tonks relational dynamic — are absent from the film with no equivalent substitution.
key_observations: Sirius Black is dead before HBP begins, so his presence in the book is entirely posthumous — yet it is substantial and multifunctional. The books use his death to drive plot (the will, Kreacher, Grimmauld Place), deepen Harry's emotional arc (grief, motivation, the parental bond), complicate other relationships (Tonks, Snape, Kreacher), and add retrospective nuance to his character (recklessness, love, family legacy). The film corpus for HBP reduces all of this to a single Slughorn throwaway line — accurate but almost entirely without consequence. The score of 54 reflects that what little the film shows is not inaccurate, but the volume and significance of what is absent is too great to permit a higher score. Confidence is medium rather than low because the book material is rich and the film's omissions are clearly identifiable, but the film corpus itself is so sparse on Sirius that several dimensions must be evaluated primarily from absence rather than from distortion.
